news_summary: Today's positive news highlights breakthroughs in historical discoveries, insights into strengthening family bonds, and surprising archaeological revelations that challenge previous assumptions.
news_summary_fr: Les bonnes nouvelles d'aujourd'hui mettent en lumière des avancées majeures dans le domaine des découvertes historiques, des idées pour renforcer les liens familiaux et des révélations archéologiques surprenantes qui remettent en question les hypothèses précédentes.
news_summary_es: Las noticias positivas de hoy destacan avances en descubrimientos históricos, ideas para fortalecer los lazos familiares y sorprendentes hallazgos arqueológicos que cuestionan las hipótesis anteriores.

articles.title: Digging Back in Time in the UAE
articles.summary: Once below a shallow sea, Jabal al Fāyah now stands above the desert in the United Arab Emirates as a reminder of a watery past and early human survival.
articles.content:
Jabal al Fāyah rises from the Rub’ al Khali desert in an image captured by the OLI (Operational Land Imager) on Landsat 8 on October 23, 2025. NASA Earth Observatory / Lauren Dauphin

About an hour’s drive east of Dubai’s gleaming towers and artificial islands, a quieter, more natural landscape takes shape. At the far northern edge of the Rub’ al Khali, a saffron-colored sand sea laps against the Al-Hajar Mountains. A series of pale ridges rises finlike from the desert plain, with the largest—Jabal al Fāyah—standing 412 meters (1,352 feet) above sea level.

The Landsat 8 satellite captured this image of the ridges cutting across the Emirate of Sharjah in the northern part of the United Arab Emirates on October 23, 2025. To geologists, the limestone ridges are a reminder of the region's watery past, signs that this land lay underwater tens of millions of years ago when the sedimentary rock layers were deposited.

Jabal al Fāyah functions as a barrier, trapping windblown sand in dune fields to its west. The weathering of iron-bearing minerals in the sand grains gives the dune fields their orange hue. To the east, the branching channels of overlapping alluvial fans extending from the Al-Hajar Mountains carry gravels and eroded sediments from basalts and other dark mafic rocks.

The dark rocks to the east—part of the Samail Ophiolite—are known to geologists for being among the world's largest, best-preserved, and most accessible exposures of ancient oceanic lithosphere, the rigid outer layer of Earth that includes both the crust and upper mantle. Oceanic lithosphere like this is normally subducted and recycled back into the mantle when tectonic plates collide. But in this area, a large section from beneath the Tethys Sea was scraped off and thrust onto the Arabian plate in a process called obduction.

Dubai lies to the west of the limestone ridges, and the Al-Hajar Mountains lie to the east, in an image acquired by the OLI (Operational Land Imager) on Landsat 8 on October 23, 2025. NASA Earth Observatory / Lauren Dauphin

The Jabal al Fāyah ridges themselves are made up of marine limestone that was deposited on top of the ophiolite over tens of millions of years spanning the late Cretaceous through the early to mid-Paleocene. Limestone typically forms along continental margins in warm, shallow oceans, often in lagoons and coral reefs, out of the calcium carbonate found in the shells and skeletons of marine life. In many parts of the ridges, coral fragments and marine invertebrate fossils are visible embedded in the rock. A feature called Fossil Rock sits a few kilometers north of Jabal al Fāyah and adjacent to the limestone ridge Jabal Mulayḩah. It contains an abundance of snail, clam, and sea urchin remains.

For archaeologists, the ridges are at the center of a much more recent tale of human adaptation and survival that has played out in just the past few hundred thousand years. The ridges and parts of the surrounding landscape—inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage site in 2025—are dotted with dozens of archaeological sites that trace human occupation on the Arabian Peninsula back to between 210,000 and 120,000 years ago, to the Middle Paleolithic. That was a period when waves of anatomically modern humans (Homo sapiens) migrated out of Africa and shared the planet with other groups such as Neanderthals.

Many of the sites contain stone flakes, blades, scrapers, hand axes, and other stone tools. The archaeological treasure trove offers early evidence of modern humans surviving in a harsh desert environment and raises questions about the routes modern Homo sapiens may have taken on their journey out of Africa.

Geological evidence indicates that lakes periodically formed on the east side of the ridge, providing critical food and water resources that would have supported early inhabitants in this unforgiving climate. Rocky overhangs along the ridge would have provided shelter from the heat and wind. Some of the sites show evidence of intermittent occupation beginning as early as 210,000 years ago, making this one of the earliest signs of human habitation on the Arabian Peninsula.

NASA Earth Observatory images by Lauren Dauphin, using Landsat data from the U.S. Geological Survey. Story by Adam Voiland.

Downloads October 23, 2025

References & Resources
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the discovery and preservation of a significant archaeological and geological site in the UAE that provides valuable insights into early human survival and migration. This has broad scientific and cultural significance, enriching our understanding of human history and adaptation in harsh environments. The designation as a UNESCO World Heritage site further underscores its global importance and positive impact on heritage conservation.
articles.personality_title: Ancient landscape in the UAE reveals early human survival and migration
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Jabal al Fāyah is a group of limestone ridges rising from the Rub’ al Khali desert in the United Arab Emirates. This area was once under a shallow sea millions of years ago and now holds important clues about Earth's past and early human life.

**What happened** – On October 23, 2025, NASA’s Landsat 8 satellite captured images showing the ridges and surrounding landscape. Scientists studying these rocks found fossils of sea creatures and signs that this land was once underwater. Archaeologists discovered many stone tools and evidence that early modern humans lived here between 210,000 and 120,000 years ago. The site was named a UNESCO World Heritage site in 2025.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows how early humans survived in a tough desert environment. The presence of lakes and shelters around the ridges would have provided water and protection. The tools and fossils help scientists understand the routes humans took when they first left Africa. It also preserves a rare geological record of ancient ocean floors pushed onto land.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue to study the area to learn more about early human migration and how people adapted to desert life. The UNESCO status will help protect the site and encourage future research and education about this key part of human history.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Jabal al Fāyah in the UAE offers valuable evidence of early human life and Earth's ancient seas, deepening our knowledge of history and survival in harsh environments.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un paysage ancien aux Émirats révèle la survie et la migration humaines anciennes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Jabal al Fāyah est un groupe de crêtes calcaires qui s'élèvent du désert de Rub’ al Khali aux Émirats arabes unis. Cette région était autrefois sous une mer peu profonde il y a des millions d'années et contient maintenant d'importants indices sur le passé de la Terre et la vie humaine ancienne.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Le 23 octobre 2025, le satellite Landsat 8 de la NASA a capturé des images montrant les crêtes et le paysage environnant. Les scientifiques ont trouvé des fossiles d'animaux marins et des signes que cette terre était autrefois sous l'eau. Les archéologues ont découvert de nombreux outils en pierre et des preuves que les premiers humains modernes y ont vécu entre 210 000 et 120 000 ans. Le site a été inscrit au patrimoine mondial de l'UNESCO en 2025.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre comment les premiers humains ont survécu dans un environnement désertique difficile. La présence de lacs et d'abris autour des crêtes aurait fourni de l'eau et une protection. Les outils et les fossiles aident les scientifiques à comprendre les routes empruntées par les humains lors de leurs premières sorties d'Afrique. Elle préserve aussi un rare témoignage géologique d'anciens fonds océaniques poussés sur la terre.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs continueront d'étudier la zone pour mieux comprendre la migration humaine ancienne et comment les humains se sont adaptés à la vie dans le désert. Le statut UNESCO aidera à protéger le site et encouragera la recherche et l'éducation futures sur cette partie clé de l'histoire humaine.

**Phrase clé** – Jabal al Fāyah aux Émirats arabes unis offre des preuves précieuses de la vie humaine ancienne et des mers anciennes de la Terre, approfondissant notre connaissance de l'histoire et de la survie dans des environnements difficiles.
articles.personality_title_es: Paisaje antiguo en Emiratos revela la supervivencia y migración humana temprana
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Jabal al Fāyah es un conjunto de crestas de piedra caliza que se elevan desde el desierto de Rub’ al Khali en los Emiratos Árabes Unidos. Esta área estuvo cubierta por un mar poco profundo hace millones de años y ahora contiene pistas importantes sobre el pasado de la Tierra y la vida humana antigua.

**Qué pasó** – El 23 de octubre de 2025, el satélite Landsat 8 de la NASA capturó imágenes que muestran las crestas y el paisaje alrededor. Los científicos encontraron fósiles de animales marinos y señales de que esta tierra estuvo bajo el agua. Arqueólogos descubrieron muchas herramientas de piedra y evidencia de que humanos modernos vivieron aquí entre hace 210,000 y 120,000 años. El sitio fue declarado Patrimonio Mundial por la UNESCO en 2025.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque muestra cómo los primeros humanos sobrevivieron en un ambiente desértico difícil. La presencia de lagos y refugios cerca de las crestas habría ofrecido agua y protección. Las herramientas y fósiles ayudan a entender las rutas que tomaron los humanos cuando salieron de África. También preserva un raro registro geológico de antiguos fondos oceánicos empujados a la tierra.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán estudiando el área para aprender más sobre la migración humana temprana y cómo las personas se adaptaron a la vida en el desierto. El estatus de la UNESCO ayudará a proteger el sitio y fomentará futuras investigaciones y educación sobre esta parte clave de la historia humana.

**Frase clave** – Jabal al Fāyah en los Emiratos Árabes Unidos ofrece evidencia valiosa de la vida humana temprana y los antiguos mares de la Tierra, ampliando nuestro conocimiento de la historia y la supervivencia en ambientes difíciles.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of the saffron-hued Jabal al Fāyah limestone ridges rising sharply from the orange sand dunes of the Rub’ al Khali desert, with subtle embedded coral and fossil textures visible in the rock faces; to the east, dark basaltic mountains and branching alluvial fans stretch beneath a soft golden sky, while faint abstract shapes of ancient stone tools and shelter-like rocky overhangs are gently integrated into the landscape, symbolizing early human survival and migration.

articles.title: The parenting habit that builds lifelong closeness with adult children
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM Picture two parents, both devoted. Both called every Sunday. Both showed up for birthdays, sent money when things got hard, and made every visible effort. One of them has an adult child who calls with the hard stuff: the job that fell apart, the relationship that ended badly, the […]
The post The parenting habit that builds lifelong closeness with adult children first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

Picture two parents, both devoted. Both called every Sunday. Both showed up for birthdays, sent money when things got hard, and made every visible effort. One of them has an adult child who calls with the hard stuff: the job that fell apart, the relationship that ended badly, the decision they’re still not sure about. The other gets the highlights.

That difference doesn’t come from one conversation. It builds over years, in moments that didn’t feel significant while they were happening.

The editing that starts early

Children read signals long before they can name them. They can sense, without being explicitly told, when the relationship is easier if things are going well. A tone of voice after disappointing news. A pause that goes a beat too long. The message doesn’t need to be said outright. This relationship has terms.

A 2022 meta-analysis by Haines and Schutte in the journal Social Development found that parental conditional regard, meaning love and approval that depends on the child meeting expectations, is consistently linked to lower self-esteem, higher anxiety, and more difficulty with closeness. Not just in childhood. Those patterns tend to deepen once the child has left home, when each interaction carries more weight, not less.

So the adult child starts editing. They bring the finished version: the decisions that worked out, the stories that won’t cause worry. The Sunday calls still happen. The relationship just stops holding the whole person. A parent can call every Sunday for thirty years and still be the person their child composes themselves for before answering.

What the research says on the other side

Dr. Robert Brooks, a clinical psychologist affiliated with Harvard Medical School and McLean Hospital, writes about this with some directness: “A lack of acceptance and unconditional love, conveyed with a message of parental disappointment during childhood, continues to negatively influence our adult relationships and lives.”

He’s writing about adults. How a parent responded to imperfection years ago doesn’t disappear. It shapes how that person navigates every close relationship, including the one with the parent.

The other direction is documented just as clearly. “Accepting and loving our children unconditionally fosters positive parent-child bonds, emotional growth, and resilience,” Brooks writes. The adult child who grew up with that knows the relationship can hold the real version of their life, not just the polished one.

What it looks like in practice

None of this needs a speech.

The parents whose adult children stay close tend to absorb bad news without making the child regret sharing it. When a job falls apart or a relationship ends, they don’t make the child feel foolish for having tried. The response doesn’t have to be perfect. It just can’t be the kind that gets filed away as: don’t bring this to them.

They apologize when they’re wrong. Not dramatically, just as a normal part of how the relationship works. The child doesn’t have to manage the parent’s ego or pretend things went differently than they did.

And they make genuine room for the child’s life to look different from what they would have chosen. Different values, different timelines, a different way of doing things. When that doesn’t carry an undertow of disappointment, the adult child can actually be themselves in the room.

The feeling underneath it all

Families that stay close don’t usually have a story about why. No single moment. What adult children in those families tend to describe is something harder to put words to: a sense that, whatever state they arrived in, the welcome didn’t come with conditions attached.

That doesn’t get built in a conversation. It accumulates in small moments, across years, in all the exchanges nobody thought to mark.

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article highlights research-backed parenting habits that foster unconditional love and lifelong emotional closeness between parents and adult children, which has broad positive implications for family well-being and mental health. It provides substantive insights and practical advice grounded in psychological studies, promoting healthier relationships and emotional resilience at a societal level.
articles.personality_title: How unconditional love builds lasting bonds between parents and adult children
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many parents try to stay close with their grown-up children by calling often and being there for special occasions. But sometimes, even with these efforts, adult children only share the good news and hide their struggles.

**What happened** – Research shows that when parents love and accept their children no matter what, without setting conditions, the children feel safe to share the hard parts of their lives too. This kind of unconditional love helps build real closeness that lasts into adulthood. On the other hand, when love feels conditional—based on meeting expectations—children often hold back and only show a polished version of themselves.

**Impact** – This finding explains why some families stay emotionally close even after children grow up, while others don’t. It highlights that small moments of acceptance, like listening without judgment or apologizing when wrong, add up over time. These habits allow adult children to be themselves around their parents, which supports emotional health and trust.

**What's next step** – Parents can focus on showing acceptance in everyday moments, especially when their children share difficulties. This approach doesn’t require big talks, just steady kindness and understanding. Over time, it can strengthen family bonds and help adult children feel truly supported.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Unconditional love and acceptance from parents create lasting emotional closeness by making adult children feel safe to share their whole lives, not just the good parts.
articles.personality_title_fr: Comment l'amour inconditionnel renforce les liens durables entre parents et enfants adultes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Beaucoup de parents essaient de rester proches de leurs enfants adultes en les appelant souvent et en étant présents lors des occasions spéciales. Mais parfois, malgré ces efforts, les enfants adultes ne partagent que les bonnes nouvelles et cachent leurs difficultés.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des recherches montrent que lorsque les parents aiment et acceptent leurs enfants sans conditions, ceux-ci se sentent en sécurité pour partager aussi les moments difficiles de leur vie. Ce type d'amour inconditionnel aide à construire une vraie proximité qui dure à l'âge adulte. En revanche, lorsque l'amour semble conditionnel—basé sur le respect des attentes—les enfants retiennent des parties d’eux-mêmes et ne montrent qu'une version embellie.

**Impact** – Cette découverte explique pourquoi certaines familles restent proches émotionnellement même après que les enfants ont grandi, tandis que d'autres non. Elle souligne que de petits moments d'acceptation, comme écouter sans juger ou s'excuser quand on a tort, s'additionnent avec le temps. Ces habitudes permettent aux enfants adultes d'être eux-mêmes avec leurs parents, ce qui soutient la santé émotionnelle et la confiance.

**Prochaine étape** – Les parents peuvent se concentrer sur l'expression de l'acceptation dans les moments quotidiens, surtout quand leurs enfants partagent des difficultés. Cette approche ne nécessite pas de grands discours, juste de la gentillesse et de la compréhension constantes. Avec le temps, cela peut renforcer les liens familiaux et aider les enfants adultes à se sentir vraiment soutenus.

**En une phrase** – L'amour et l'acceptation inconditionnels des parents créent une proximité émotionnelle durable en permettant aux enfants adultes de partager toute leur vie, pas seulement les bons côtés.
articles.personality_title_es: Cómo el amor incondicional fortalece los lazos duraderos entre padres e hijos adultos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchos padres intentan mantenerse cerca de sus hijos adultos llamándolos con frecuencia y estando presentes en ocasiones especiales. Pero a veces, a pesar de estos esfuerzos, los hijos adultos solo comparten las buenas noticias y ocultan sus dificultades.

**Qué pasó** – Las investigaciones muestran que cuando los padres aman y aceptan a sus hijos sin condiciones, estos se sienten seguros para compartir también las partes difíciles de sus vidas. Este tipo de amor incondicional ayuda a crear una verdadera cercanía que dura en la edad adulta. En cambio, cuando el amor parece condicional—basado en cumplir expectativas—los hijos tienden a reservarse y solo muestran una versión pulida de sí mismos.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo explica por qué algunas familias mantienen una cercanía emocional incluso después de que los hijos crecen, mientras que otras no. Destaca que pequeños momentos de aceptación, como escuchar sin juzgar o disculparse al equivocarse, se suman con el tiempo. Estas costumbres permiten que los hijos adultos sean ellos mismos con sus padres, lo que apoya la salud emocional y la confianza.

**Próximo paso** – Los padres pueden enfocarse en mostrar aceptación en los momentos cotidianos, especialmente cuando sus hijos comparten dificultades. Este enfoque no requiere grandes conversaciones, solo amabilidad y comprensión constantes. Con el tiempo, puede fortalecer los lazos familiares y ayudar a los hijos adultos a sentirse realmente apoyados.

**Resumen en una frase** – El amor y la aceptación incondicional de los padres crean una cercanía emocional duradera al permitir que los hijos adultos compartan toda su vida, no solo las partes buenas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of two intertwined, gently glowing open hands—one larger, one smaller—cradling a delicate, unfolding origami heart made of layered paper, set against a soft, natural palette of muted earth tones and warm neutrals, symbolizing unconditional love, lifelong closeness, and the quiet strength of accepting imperfection across time.

articles.title: Everyone thought these helmets were Roman until scientists uncovered the truth
articles.summary: Researchers have solved a decades-old mystery by showing that a cache of 43 helmets found off the Spanish coast is medieval, not Roman. The remarkable discovery exposes a thriving weapons trade network that connected Mediterranean powers during a time of piracy, warfare, and growing demand for military equipment.
articles.content:
For more than 30 years, a remarkable collection of helmets recovered from the waters off Spain was believed to date back to Roman times. New research has now overturned that assumption, revealing that the artifacts are actually medieval and provide rare insight into the movement of weapons, trade, and military activity across the Mediterranean during the Late Middle Ages.

The study, led by researchers at the University of Alicante (UA), reexamined 43 helmets discovered in 1990 at the underwater archaeological site of Piedras de la Barbada near Benicarló on Spain's eastern coast. The findings, published in the Cambridge University Press journal Antiquity, show that the helmets were manufactured between the late 14th and early 15th centuries, completely overturning their long-standing Roman classification.

The research was led by Manuel Frallicciardi, a doctoral student jointly supervised by the University of Alicante and the University of Salerno.

Largest Medieval Helmet Hoard in the Western Mediterranean

The discovery itself happened by accident. Local fishermen pulled up two large masses of metal that had become fused together through centuries of marine corrosion after snagging them in their nets. Inside the concreted blocks was an extraordinary cache of iron helmets.

Although archaeologists believe the original shipment may have contained even more pieces, the surviving collection of 43 helmets already represents the largest known hoard of medieval helmets ever found in the western Mediterranean.

According to Raimon Graells, a lecturer at the University of Alicante, co-director of Frallicciardi's doctoral project, and co-author of the study, the significance of the find extends well beyond the artifacts themselves.

"We are looking at direct evidence of large-scale arms trading. This discovery reveals a network of exchange and communication that was far more complex than previously thought," he said.

The findings point to active movement of military equipment between the coast of what is now the Valencia Region and major commercial centers in northern Italy, including Genoa, one of the era's most powerful trading hubs. Researchers say the size of the shipment indicates that weapons were being transported through well-established commercial systems linking different parts of the Mediterranean.

New Analysis Reveals a Medieval Origin

One of the study's most important advances was the use of an analytical method developed at the University of Alicante. While the technique has been used successfully in other archaeological investigations, it had never before been applied to medieval weapons of this type.

Combined with radiocarbon dating of fabric fragments preserved inside several helmets, the approach allowed researchers to establish a highly accurate age for the artifacts.

Frallicciardi explained that identifying the helmets initially proved difficult.

"At the beginning, it was difficult to place them in a specific era because they featured traits that recalled both Late Roman models and potential medieval pieces inspired by classical traditions," he said.

The results became even more surprising when researchers discovered that the helmets did not match any previously documented category.

"When I started the research, it was incredible to see that practically no known parallels existed," Frallicciardi said.

While searching for comparisons, he found a few similar helmet depictions in 14th-century English artwork, but no exact equivalents. Carbon-14 dating ultimately confirmed that the helmets belonged to a poorly documented design from a transitional period in military technology that left no direct descendants.

A Lost Shipment Preserved Under the Seafloor

Researchers believe all 43 helmets were originally part of a single cargo. The most likely explanation is that the shipment was being loaded or unloaded when an accident caused it to fall into the water.

The site lies only about six meters (20 feet) deep and is located beside an area that served as a jetty.

Graells suggested that part of the cargo may have become buried beneath the sand immediately after the accident, preventing recovery efforts at the time. As a result, the shipment remained hidden for centuries.

The helmets survived in exceptional condition thanks to a combination of sediment and mineral deposits that formed around them underwater. In several cases, these concretions sealed off the interior lining, preserving pieces of fabric that would normally have decayed long ago.

Those textile fragments ultimately became one of the most important sources of evidence in reconstructing the history and age of the collection.

Piracy, Warfare, and Demand for Weapons

The researchers believe the shipment sank during a particularly unstable period in Mediterranean history.

During the mid-14th century, Islamic piracy expanded along the Valencian coastline, while increasing militarization created growing demand for protective equipment and weapons. In that environment, the helmets may have been intended for local militias, forces serving the Kingdom of Valencia, or armed groups responsible for defending the region's maritime frontier.

Far from being Roman relics, the helmets now offer rare evidence of medieval trade, military logistics, and the movement of arms across one of the world's most important commercial regions.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant archaeological discovery that overturns long-held assumptions about a large cache of helmets, revealing new insights into medieval arms trade and military history in the Mediterranean. This has broad cultural and historical significance, enriching our understanding of past societies and trade networks, which is inspiring and intellectually uplifting for the general public.
articles.personality_title: Medieval helmets found off Spain rewrite Mediterranean history
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For over 30 years, a group of helmets found underwater off Spain was thought to be Roman. These helmets were discovered in 1990 near Benicarló on Spain’s eastern coast and were believed to be ancient relics from Roman times.

**What happened** – New research by the University of Alicante showed that these helmets are actually from the late 14th to early 15th centuries, meaning they are medieval, not Roman. The helmets were part of a large shipment that accidentally sank near a jetty. There are 43 helmets in total, making this the largest collection of medieval helmets found in the western Mediterranean.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it reveals a complex trade network for weapons across the Mediterranean during the Late Middle Ages. The helmets show that military gear was moved between places like Valencia in Spain and Genoa in Italy, two major trading centers. The helmets also represent a rare design that had not been documented before, giving new information about medieval military technology.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue studying the helmets and the trade routes they represent. This could help historians learn more about how medieval armies got their weapons and how different Mediterranean regions worked together during times of piracy and warfare.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Helmets once thought Roman are actually medieval, revealing a lost shipment that shines new light on arms trade and military life in the Mediterranean centuries ago.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des casques médiévaux découverts au large de l’Espagne réécrivent l’histoire méditerranéenne
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Pendant plus de 30 ans, un groupe de casques retrouvés sous l’eau au large de l’Espagne était considéré comme romain. Ces casques ont été découverts en 1990 près de Benicarló, sur la côte est de l’Espagne, et étaient censés être des reliques de l’époque romaine.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une nouvelle recherche menée par l’Université d’Alicante a montré que ces casques datent en réalité de la fin du XIVe au début du XVe siècle, donc du Moyen Âge et non de l’époque romaine. Les casques faisaient partie d’un grand chargement qui a coulé accidentellement près d’une jetée. Il y a 43 casques au total, ce qui en fait la plus grande collection de casques médiévaux trouvée en Méditerranée occidentale.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle révèle un réseau complexe de commerce d’armes en Méditerranée durant la fin du Moyen Âge. Les casques montrent que l’équipement militaire circulait entre des lieux comme Valence en Espagne et Gênes en Italie, deux grands centres commerciaux. Ces casques représentent aussi un modèle rare, jamais documenté auparavant, donnant de nouvelles informations sur la technologie militaire médiévale.

**Prochaines étapes** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à étudier les casques et les routes commerciales qu’ils représentent. Cela pourrait aider les historiens à mieux comprendre comment les armées médiévales s’approvisionnaient en armes et comment les régions méditerranéennes collaboraient en période de piraterie et de guerre.

**En une phrase** – Des casques autrefois considérés comme romains sont en fait médiévaux, révélant un chargement perdu qui éclaire le commerce d’armes et la vie militaire en Méditerranée il y a plusieurs siècles.
articles.personality_title_es: Cascos medievales encontrados frente a España reescriben la historia mediterránea
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante más de 30 años, un grupo de cascos encontrados bajo el agua frente a España se creyó que eran romanos. Estos cascos fueron descubiertos en 1990 cerca de Benicarló, en la costa este de España, y se pensaba que eran reliquias de la época romana.

**Qué pasó** – Una nueva investigación de la Universidad de Alicante mostró que estos cascos son en realidad de finales del siglo XIV y principios del XV, es decir, medievales y no romanos. Los cascos formaban parte de un gran cargamento que se hundió accidentalmente cerca de un muelle. Hay 43 cascos en total, lo que representa la mayor colección de cascos medievales encontrada en el Mediterráneo occidental.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque revela una red compleja de comercio de armas en el Mediterráneo durante la Baja Edad Media. Los cascos muestran que el equipo militar se movía entre lugares como Valencia en España y Génova en Italia, dos importantes centros comerciales. Además, representan un diseño raro que no se había documentado antes, aportando nueva información sobre la tecnología militar medieval.

**Próximos pasos** – Los investigadores continuarán estudiando los cascos y las rutas comerciales que representan. Esto podría ayudar a los historiadores a entender mejor cómo los ejércitos medievales obtenían sus armas y cómo las regiones mediterráneas colaboraban en tiempos de piratería y guerra.

**Una frase resumen** – Cascos que se creían romanos son medievales, revelando un cargamento perdido que arroja nueva luz sobre el comercio de armas y la vida militar en el Mediterráneo hace siglos.
